mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Nigeria on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, on the west coast, with a coastline on the Gulf of Guinea (Atlantic Ocean) 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Nigeria?
mapQuestions.answer: Abuja, not Lagos! Lagos is the biggest city by far (about 20 million people), but Abuja is the official capital, in the centre of the country.

mapQuestions.question: How big is Nigeria?
mapQuestions.answer: Nigeria has about 220 million people, the most of any country in Africa, and the 6th most in the whole world. About 1 in every 5 Africans is Nigerian.

mapQuestions.question: How many languages does Nigeria have?
mapQuestions.answer: Over 500! English is the official language, and the three biggest local ones are Hausa, Yoruba and Igbo. Nigerian Pidgin (a creative mix of English and local languages) is spoken by tens of millions too.

mapQuestions.question: Why is the country called "Nigeria"?
mapQuestions.answer: After the River Niger, which flows through the country into the Atlantic. The name "Nigeria" was suggested by a British journalist in 1897 and stuck.

foods.name: Jollof rice
foods.description: Bright orange-red tomato-and-pepper rice, cooked in one pot with chicken or fish stock. Nigerians and Ghanaians ARGUE constantly about who makes the best jollof. Eaten at every party.

foods.name: Egusi soup
foods.description: A thick stew of ground melon seeds, leafy greens, palm oil and meat or fish. Eaten by scooping it up with pounded yam or fufu, using your hands.

foods.name: Suya
foods.description: Thinly sliced beef rubbed in a spicy peanut-and-chilli powder called "yaji", grilled over coals on a stick. The famous Nigerian street BBQ, sold from carts at night.

foods.name: Akara
foods.description: Deep-fried bean cakes made from black-eyed peas, ground with chilli and onion. Eaten for breakfast with pap (millet porridge) or as a snack.

foods.name: Pounded yam
foods.description: Boiled white yam pounded in a wooden mortar until it becomes a soft, smooth dough. Eaten with stews and soups, never with cutlery, always with your right hand.

foods.name: Puff puff
foods.description: Sweet round fried-dough balls dusted with sugar. Sold everywhere as a snack and at every Nigerian celebration.

scavengerItems.label: A huge open-air market full of colour (Lagos's markets are legendary)
A yellow-and-black "danfo" minibus, the iconic Lagos public transport
Afrobeats music playing somewhere nearby (almost guaranteed)
A Super Eagles (Nigerian national team) football jersey
Bright "Ankara" wax-print or "aso oke" woven fabric clothing
A traditional Yoruba or Igbo carved mask
A Nollywood movie poster or DVD stand
A "keke napep" (yellow tricycle taxi) carrying more people than seems possible
A hibiscus or "zobo" juice stand (deep red, sweet, completely non-alcoholic)
A "talking drum" being played, an hourglass-shaped drum that mimics speech
The Lekki Conservation Centre treetop canopy walk in Lagos
Zuma Rock outside Abuja or Olumo Rock in Abeokuta (giant single-stone monoliths)

geoMatches.description: The biggest city in Africa, about 20 million people, packed onto a stretch of lagoon and islands. Loud, fast, creative, exhausting. The heart of Afrobeats and Nollywood.
The capital, in the centre of the country. Quieter and more planned than Lagos, with the giant Aso Rock looming over it.
The south-west, home of the Yoruba people and culture. Beaded crowns, hidden palaces, talking drums, and the cities of Ibadan and Ile-Ife.
A coastal city in the south-east, famous for the biggest street carnival in Africa each December, brought to life with massive parade floats.
A vast network of swamps, mangroves and creeks where the Niger River reaches the Atlantic. Home to many small ethnic groups and a huge variety of wildlife.
A 725-metre rock monolith just outside Abuja, looking like it has a face cut into it. The rock is on the back of the 100-naira note.

senses.smell: Suya being smoked over coals on a street corner, palm oil heating up in a pot, sweet plantain frying, the salty Atlantic breeze in Lagos, fresh hibiscus in zobo juice…
senses.hear: Afrobeats EVERYWHERE (Burna Boy, Wizkid, Tems, Davido), Nigerian Pidgin flying across markets, "Wetin dey?", danfo conductors hanging out of doors shouting "Obalende! Obalende!", talking drums, the rumble of Lagos generators…
senses.taste: Smoky peppery suya, rich orange jollof, sweet doughy puff puff, tangy hibiscus juice, the warmth of pounded yam dipped in egusi…
senses.feel: Humid Lagos heat, the press of a huge market crowd, soft Ankara fabric, the chunky weight of beaded jewellery, the bass of a sound system in your chest…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why is Nigerian movie-making so huge?
stories.body: "Nollywood" is Nigeria's film industry, and it is the second-biggest in the world by number of films released each year, behind only India's Bollywood. Nollywood started in the early 1990s when a Lagos electronics trader bought a bunch of leftover blank video tapes and decided to record short films onto them to sell. The first big hit was "Living in Bondage" in 1992. Today Nollywood releases around 2,500 films a year and employs more than a million people. The films are loved across Africa and by African diaspora communities everywhere, and the bigger releases now show on Netflix and Amazon Prime.

stories.question: What are the "jollof wars"?
stories.body: Jollof rice is a tomato-and-pepper one-pot rice dish loved across West Africa, but every country claims theirs is the BEST. The biggest argument is between Nigeria and Ghana, who go to war over jollof on social media almost every day. Senegal actually invented it (the original name is "thieboudienne"), but they don't shout about it as loudly. Each country uses different rice (Nigeria favours long-grain, Ghana basmati), different spice mixes, different smoke levels at the bottom of the pot (Nigerians love it slightly burnt for the "party jollof" flavour). It is a totally serious culinary debate. Try both. Decide for yourself.

stories.question: Why does Nigeria have over 500 languages?
stories.body: Nigeria sits at a crossroads where several big African language families meet. Long before any modern country existed, hundreds of small ethnic groups developed their own languages in their own valleys, plains, and forests. When the British drew Nigeria's borders in 1914, they grouped all of those peoples into one country. Today the three biggest groups are Hausa (in the north), Yoruba (in the south-west) and Igbo (in the south-east), each with tens of millions of speakers. But hundreds of smaller languages are still alive. To bridge them all, Nigerians use English (the official language) and Nigerian Pidgin (an English-based creole that is now its own beautiful, expressive language).

stories.question: What are Afrobeats and who is Fela Kuti?
stories.body: "Afrobeats" is the bright, dance-driven sound that has taken over global pop music in the last decade. Wizkid, Burna Boy, Tems, Davido, Rema, all Nigerian. But it grew out of an older sound called "Afrobeat" (no "s"), invented in the 1970s by a Lagos saxophonist called Fela Kuti. Fela mixed funk, jazz, Yoruba drumming and political protest into long, hypnotic songs, and ran a Lagos club called the Shrine where the band would play for hours. Fela's sons (Femi and Seun) still play that older Afrobeat sound today. Modern Afrobeats is faster and more pop-shaped, but the lineage runs straight back to Fela.

stories.question: Who was Wole Soyinka?
stories.body: Wole Soyinka, born in 1934 in Abeokuta, is a Nigerian playwright and poet who in 1986 became the FIRST African writer ever to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. His plays, poems and memoirs draw heavily on Yoruba traditional theatre and mythology, and he writes about colonialism, freedom and corruption. Soyinka has been jailed for his writing and exiled for it too, and at over 90 years old he is still writing and speaking publicly. Chinua Achebe (the author of "Things Fall Apart", another giant of African literature) was also Nigerian, born in Igbo country in the south-east, in the same generation.

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Nigeria so far?
Have you tried saying "no wahala" to anyone?
Lagos, Abuja, markets, coast, which has felt the most "Nigeria"?
Have you heard an Afrobeats song you really love this trip?
How would you feel about living in a city of 20 million people?
Nigeria has over 500 languages. How would you choose which one to learn first?

wordSearch.hint: Nigerian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Mona monkey
animals.description: Common in southern forests and even some Lagos parks. Olive-brown with a long tail.

animals.name: Senegal parrot
animals.description: Bright green and yellow, often kept as a pet but also seen flying wild across the savanna.

animals.name: Nile crocodile
animals.description: In the rivers and lagoons of the Niger Delta and the inland rivers.

animals.name: African buffalo
animals.description: In Cross River and Yankari national parks. Heavy, dangerous, usually in herds.

animals.name: Hippo
animals.description: In the Niger River and the rivers of Cross River state. Dangerous, give them space.

animals.name: Forest elephant
animals.description: Smaller than savanna elephants and very rare. The last refuge is the Cross River rainforest.

animals.name: Cross River gorilla
animals.description: The rarest gorilla on Earth, fewer than 300 left, only in the Nigerian-Cameroonian border forests.
